one_line_takeaway: JCB shows that a black-box jailbreak attacker can skip expensive auxiliary-LLM-based prompt rewriting and still substantially outperform PAIR/TAP/GCG-family baselines, by cheaply perturbing a small fixed seed set with WordNet synonym substitution and sharing which seeds worked across different harmful behaviors, cutting queries by up to 94% while raising average HarmBench ASR by about 12%.
key_finding: On HarmBench (Table 1, open-source models), JCB achieves an average ASR of 60.95, beating the next-best baseline GCG (54.3 ASR, a white-box gradient method) by about 12%, and outperforms essentially all black-box baselines on nearly every target model; notably it reaches 37.0% ASR on Llama-2-7B-Chat, described as one of the most resilient open models. Against recent closed/frontier models (Table 2) JCB gets 76.75% ASR on GPT-3.5-Turbo-1106 (61.5% higher than runner-up TAP-T's 47.5%), 86.25% on DeepSeek-V3.1, 42.25% on GPT-4o, 24.5% on GPT-4.1, but only 8.75% on Claude-3.7-Sonnet and 10.25% on Claude-3.5-Haiku, indicating the Anthropic models were comparatively resistant. On query efficiency (Table 3, average queries to success across five Llama-2/3 models), JCB needs only 3.42 queries on average versus 63.62 for PAIR, 59.18 for TAP, and 8.51 for AutoDAN-Turbo (Gemma variant) -- a 94% reduction versus PAIR/TAP and 59% versus AutoDAN-Turbo. Under an iso-query comparison at 4000 queries (Table 5), JCB's average ASR of 26.7% is 184% higher than AutoDAN-Turbo's 9.4%, though AutoDAN-Turbo (which the authors classify as a separate, much higher-budget 'high-complexity' method using up to 360,000 queries) still reaches a higher absolute ASR (84.7% average, per the paper's own comparison) when given its full unconstrained query budget -- JCB trades 24 ASR points for two orders of magnitude fewer queries. Zero-shot transfer (Table 4, ATR metric) shows meaningful cross-model transfer of successful prompts, e.g. from Llama-2-7B-Chat averaging 54.7% ATR across 12 other target models. On AdvBench, JCB reportedly reaches 91.2% average ASR and outperforms additional baselines there by over 33% (appendix-level, not reproduced with full tables in the pages reviewed).
core_contribution: The paper introduces JCB (Jailbreak with Cross-Behavior attacks), a black-box, automated jailbreak method for LLMs built from four components: (1) a one-time Seed Prompt Generation step that uses an LLM to write elaborate stories around the two most successful jailbreak themes identified by prior work ('assumed responsibility' and 'character roleplay'); (2) a Selection component that picks a seed prompt via weighted-random sampling proportional to each seed's historical success; (3) a Perturbation component that performs lightweight, part-of-speech-preserving synonym substitution (via WordNet/NLTK) on the selected prompt instead of invoking an auxiliary LLM, which the authors measure to be over 400x faster than an LLM-based paraphraser (GPT-3.5-Turbo-0125) while still producing successful jailbreaks; and (4) an Execution and Evaluation component that queries the target LLM and scores the response with a separate scorer LLM (score 1-10, threshold Psi=8.5). The key novelty the authors claim is cross-behavior weight sharing: when a perturbed seed succeeds on one harmful behavior, its weight is increased so it is more likely to be reused (and further perturbed) for other, unrelated harmful behaviors, letting successes on one behavior bootstrap successes on others and sharply cutting the number of target-LLM queries needed.
threat_model: Fully black-box: JCB assumes access only to the target LLM's final textual output (no logits, gradients, weights, or tokenizer access), making it applicable to closed-source/API-gated models as well as open-weight ones. It relies on an auxiliary seed-generator LLM (ChatGPT-4o-Latest, invoked once to produce Ns=20 seed prompts) and an auxiliary scorer LLM (GPT-4.1-2025-04-14) to judge jailbreak success; the perturbation step itself uses no LLM, only WordNet-based synonym substitution with substitution probability p=0.5. The attacker's goal is to elicit harmful/toxic/policy-violating content from a target LLM across many distinct harmful behaviors while minimizing target-LLM query count and avoiding costly auxiliary-LLM calls per perturbation.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) average ASR, while state-of-the-art among black-box low-complexity methods, is still only about 60% and could improve with larger compute/runtime budgets; (2) the method could likely be improved further with algorithmic changes such as periodically regenerating seed prompts, left to future work; (3) evaluation is restricted to HarmBench and AdvBench, so performance may vary on other datasets; (4) the HarmBench classifier used for scoring ASR, despite ~93.19% accuracy, is not perfectly correct and may have introduced some false positives/negatives into the reported results. Additionally (from the paper's own framing, not the authors' explicit limitations list): JCB substantially underperforms on Claude-3.7-Sonnet and Claude-3.5-Haiku relative to other frontier models, and it trades off absolute attack success against AutoDAN-Turbo's unconstrained-budget performance (84.7% vs JCB's ~61% average) in exchange for far fewer queries -- i.e., JCB is optimized for efficiency, not maximum attack ceiling.

datasets_used: HarmBench textual behaviors dataset (400 malicious requests: standard, copyright, and contextual behaviors)
quantitative_results: JCB average HarmBench ASR = 60.95% across open-source LLMs (Table 1), vs second-best GCG = 54.3% (12% relative improvement)
JCB ASR on GPT-3.5-Turbo-1106 = 76.75% vs runner-up TAP-T = 47.5% (61.5% higher)
JCB ASR on DeepSeek-V3.1 = 86.25%, GPT-4o = 42.25%, GPT-4.1 = 24.5%, Claude-3.5-Haiku = 10.25%, Claude-3.7-Sonnet = 8.75%, Llama-4-Maverick-17B = 55.25% (Table 2)

Average queries to successful jailbreak (5 Llama-2/3 models, Table 3): JCB = 3.42, PAIR = 63.62, TAP = 59.18, AutoDAN-Turbo (Gemma) = 8.51 -- JCB uses 94% fewer queries than PAIR/TAP and 59% fewer than AutoDAN-Turbo
Iso-query (4000 queries) ASR comparison (Table 5): JCB average = 26.7% vs AutoDAN-Turbo = 9.4% (184% higher)
AutoDAN-Turbo full-budget average HarmBench ASR = 84.7% using up to 360,000 queries, vs JCB's ~61% average using at most 4000 queries
Zero-shot Attack Transfer Rate (ATR) from Llama-2-7B-Chat averaged 54.7% across 12 target LLMs (Table 4)
AdvBench generalization: JCB average ASR = 91.2%, outperforming additional baselines by >33% (appendix-level claim)
HarmBench classifier accuracy used for ASR scoring = 93.19% (noted as a source of possible measurement error)
